data.title: Wall Street opens higher after selloff, Middle East in focus
data.description:
Wall Street's main indexes opened higher on Monday after logging declines in the previous session, following President Donald Trump's comments on the U.S. and Iran talks even as the Middle East conflict widened.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 116.4 points, or 0.26%, at the open to 45,283.06. The S&P 500 rose 34.5 points, or 0.54%, at the open to 6,403.37​, while the Nasdaq Composite rose 147.9 points, or 0.71%, to 21,096.243 at the opening bell.

data.title: Bankinter Buys Alantra's Stake in Access Capital Partners for $132.5 Million
data.description:
By Elena Vardon

Spanish bank Bankinter said it would buy a 49% stake in Access Capital Partners from peer Alantra for 115.1 million euros ($132.5 million) as part of its alternative-investment push.

Bankinter said it will own 64% of the Paris-based private assets manager's economic rights once the deal closes. Access will continue to operate independently, with its management team holding the majority of voting rights, it said separately. The firm, which operates in six European countries, specializes in the infrastructure and private equity sectors and oversees around 9 billion euros in capital under management.

Alantra's investment management arm is set to make 69 million euros in capital gain from the disposal, it said. The group bought the Access stake from Finnish financial-services company OP Financial Group in tranches in 2018 and 2022.

On top of the sale's proceeds, Alantra has roughly 50 million euros in available cash, which it intends to deploy to acquire European specialized asset managers in order to accelerate its growth. "We have the firepower to play an active role in the consolidation of the market," it said.

Bankinter on Friday also announced the merger of its investment arm with the asset manager Plenium Partners to create a direct investment platform.

Shares in Bankinter ticked up 1% on Monday afternoon, while those of Alantra rose more than 6%, paring earlier gains of nearly 10% earlier in the session.

The closure of both deals, which represent more than 14 billion euros in capital committed by investors, is subject to regulatory approvals, Bankinter said. The Access stake transaction is expected to close in the second half of the year, Alantra said.

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: Norwegian Cruise Line Hoping For Project Payoffs — Market Talk
data.description: The recent pullback in shares of Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings makes the stock more attractive, but the impact of improved execution still needs to be seen, says Morgan Stanley in a note. The company's renovation at its Great Stirrup Cay destination is still a ways away from being completed. The pier will be closed in April through most of the summer and the water park is only likely to open in the very late summer, making contributions this year minimal. However, once the destination is in full operation, it should drive significant upside to yields with proper execution across marketing and revenue management, the analysts say.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.description:

data.title: Flex to Acquire Electrical Power Products for About $1.1 Billion
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Flex agreed to acquire Electrical Power Products in an all-cash transaction valued at roughly $1.1 billion, including about $100 million in anticipated tax benefits.

Chief Executive Officer Revathi Advaithi said Monday that the deal will expand Flex's critical power capabilities, allowing the company to play a larger role in modernizing the U.S. electrical infrastructure.

The acquisition is expected to be accretive to Flex's adjusted per-share earnings in the first full year after closing, anticipated to occur in the first quarter of Flex's fiscal 2027.

Electrical Power Products, based in Des Moines, Iowa, designs and manufactures engineered-to-order electrical power control and protection systems for utility, power generation, and industrial clients. The company is expected to generate revenue of about $323 million in its fiscal year ending March 31, with double-digit organic growth projected.

President Tim O'Donnell said joining Flex will support the company's long-term growth while maintaining its engineering-driven culture.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Aluminum Is the New Oil. These Stocks Are Soaring. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Alex Kozul-Wright

Alcoa, Century Aluminum and other mining stocks were jumping early Monday, as two major Middle Eastern aluminum producers sustained damage to their production facilities over the weekend.

Alcoa was up 8.1% to $63.12 in premarket trading. Heading into Monday's trading, the stock is down 5.9% over the past month since the Iran began. During that time, the S&P 500 index has dropped 7.4%.

Century Aluminum, down around 4% since the conflict started, pointed 8.6% higher ahead of the open. Constellium and Kaiser Aluminum were both around 2% higher.

As the war in Iran began, Alcoa shares fell alongside the broader market amid fears of slowing consumer and industrial demand linked to higher energy prices.

But it's recent rebound may have been driven by supply constrains. State-backed giants Emirates Global Aluminium and Aluminium Bahrain were attacked on Saturday, The Wall Street Journal reported.

The Middle East accounts for 9% of global aluminum production according to ANZ. In turn, the bank says four-to-five million metric tons of exports are at risk.

Indeed, aluminum forward contracts were up 4.8%, at $3,342 per metric ton, in New York early Monday, according to FactSet data. Prices are now up 10% since Feb. 27, the day before fighting began.

Looking ahead, Alcoa could benefit from stronger margins and renewed investor interest linked to supply chokeholds — turning what began as a broad selloff into a potential tailwind for the stock.

Write to Alex Kozul-Wright at alexander.kozul-wright@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
